result:
# The Cartography of Unfinished Things

I have been thinking lately about maps that don't work.

Not broken maps, exactly. Not maps with errors, though those are interesting too — the medieval cartographers who filled oceans with sea monsters not out of superstition but out of an honest acknowledgment that they simply did not know what was out there, that the blankness needed some kind of marking, that *here be dragons* was at least a theory. No, I mean maps that fail at a more fundamental level. Maps of things that resist being mapped. Maps of conversations that almost happened. Maps of the feeling you get at four in the afternoon in late October when the light goes sideways and golden through a window and you can't tell if you're happy or sad or just alive in a way that feels unusually loud.

There's a word in Portuguese — *saudade* — that people love to cite as untranslatable, a longing for something you may never have had, or something you had so long ago it has passed beyond memory into mythology. People cite it as evidence that other languages can feel things English cannot. But I think the more honest reading is that every language fails at this equally, and *saudade* is just the place where Portuguese made its failure visible, turned the inadequacy into a word, framed the defeat and hung it on the wall. English has the same feeling. English just calls it *that feeling* and moves on, embarrassed.

This is what I want to talk about: the things we can't finish mapping, and why we keep trying anyway.

---

There's a particular kind of human project that is defined entirely by its own impossibility. The novel that would contain everything. The philosophical system that would explain everything. The conversation with a parent or a child or a lost friend that would finally, finally resolve something that has been sitting unresolved for years like a stone in a shoe. These projects matter not despite the fact that they can't be completed but because of it. The impossibility is the engine.

Jorge Luis Borges wrote a story about a map drawn to the scale of 1:1 — a map as large as the territory it represented, eventually covering the entire country, eventually abandoned because it was useless, left to rot in the desert, tattered fragments overtaken by birds and foxes. People read this as satire of cartographic ambition, or philosophical commentary on representation and reality, and it is both those things. But I also read it as a love story. There is something tender in the image of those mapmakers, so devoted to accuracy that they could not bring themselves to simplify, could not choose what to leave out, kept adding detail until the project consumed itself. That's not a failure of reason. That's a recognizable feeling.

We do this with memories. We try to hold everything. We know we can't — the neuroscience is fairly brutal on this point, memory is reconstructive, every time you remember something you're actually re-remembering the last time you remembered it, a copy of a copy of a copy, signal degrading with each pass. And yet we resist compression. We take photographs not to remember but to protest against forgetting, which is a different thing. The photograph says: *this happened, and I refuse to allow it to shrink*. The photograph is a 1:1 map. The photograph is also, eventually, a box in a closet, a folder on a hard drive that gets lost in a migration, a face you can no longer name.

---

I spent a long time being bothered by the fact that I can't remember most of my life.

Not in a clinical sense — just the ordinary, devastating mathematics of experience versus retention. Think about how many hours you have been alive. Think about how many of those hours are genuinely accessible to you right now, retrievable as something more than vague emotional residue. For most people it's a tiny fraction. Years reduced to a handful of scenes. Whole relationships compressed into two or three images. You lived inside a particular Tuesday afternoon for exactly as long as a Tuesday afternoon lasts, and now it is gone as completely as if it never existed, which is to say: it still exists, it happened, causality guarantees that it mattered in ways that rippled forward through time whether or not you can access it, but to your conscious mind it is simply absent.

This used to feel like loss. It still does, sometimes. But I have been trying to think about it differently.

There is a Buddhist idea — I'm going to mangle it, I always mangle it, but here goes — that the self is not a container but a process. Not a thing that has experiences but a pattern that experiences are part of. On this view, the fact that you can't retrieve Tuesday afternoon is not a loss because there was no *you* separate from Tuesday afternoon that needed to keep it. Tuesday afternoon was the self, briefly, and then the self became Wednesday morning, and nothing was lost because there was nothing to lose. The river doesn't mourn the water that has passed downstream.

I find this both completely convincing and completely useless as comfort. Which seems right. The ideas that are completely convincing and completely useless as comfort are usually the truest ones.

---

Here is something I love: the way expertise makes the world larger instead of smaller.

There's a common version of the opposite story — the idea that knowing too much about something kills its magic, that the botanist can't enjoy a flower the way a child can, that the film student ruins movies by noticing the cuts. I don't believe this, or at least I don't think it has to be true. Every expert I have ever admired, in any field, has described the opposite experience: that the more they learned, the more intricate and strange and inexhaustible their subject became, that knowledge opened onto more knowledge the way one mountain ascended reveals five more mountains previously hidden.

The ornithologist who has spent forty years studying a single species of warbler does not have a diminished relationship with that bird. She has discovered that the bird is a continent. She knows things about its behavior, its song variations across populations, its parasites, its evolutionary history, the specific genetic switches that trigger migration, and each piece of knowledge has not simplified the bird but multiplied it, revealed layers she didn't know to look for before she knew to look for them. The child's wonder at a brightly colored wing is real and good. The ornithologist's wonder at the same wing, compounded by everything she knows about the impossibility of flight and the miracle of metabolic efficiency and the thousands of generations of selection pressure that produced that exact pigmentation, is also real and is larger.

This is the argument for caring deeply about things. Not as a productivity strategy or a path to expertise-as-status, but as a way of expanding what the world contains. You choose your subject and it rewards you by becoming infinite.

---

I want to say something about difficulty, because I think we have a confused relationship with it.

There is a version of the embrace-difficulty message that is essentially just the Protestant work ethic in philosophical clothing — *that which does not kill you makes you stronger*, *no pain no gain*, the implicit suggestion that suffering is currency and comfort is waste. I don't mean that. I am fairly skeptical of the idea that hardship is good for you in some general, automatic way. Plenty of hardship is just bad. Plenty of difficulty just wears people down without revelation.

But there is a different kind of difficulty that I think genuinely matters, which is the difficulty of sitting with open questions. The difficulty of not resolving things prematurely. This is hard in a specific way that is not about suffering but about patience and tolerance for ambiguity, and it is a skill, and it is worth cultivating.

The philosopher John Keats — actually a poet, but philosophy is wherever you find it — called this *negative capability*: the capacity to remain in uncertainty, in doubt, without any irritable reaching after fact and reason. He thought Shakespeare had it supremely. The ability to inhabit a character completely without needing to impose a verdict on them, to dramatize a point of view without resolving it into a lesson. This is what makes Hamlet Hamlet rather than a morality play. We don't know, finally, what we're supposed to conclude about him. He remains unresolved. He remains alive, in the way that only unresolved things remain alive.

I think about this in relation to the unfinished conversations I mentioned at the beginning. There is an instinct to want resolution — to have the talk, to get the closure, to know where you stand. And sometimes that instinct is healthy and necessary. But sometimes the desire for resolution is really the desire to stop feeling uncertain, which is a desire to stop a living thing in its tracks and pin it to a board. Some conversations should stay open. Some questions should stay questions. The irritable reaching after resolution kills the thing you were trying to hold.

---

There is a city I think about sometimes that I have never visited.

I have read about it, looked at photographs, heard it described by people I trust. I have a relationship with this city that is entirely imaginary, which means I have a relationship with a city of my own construction — I have assembled it from fragments and projections and filled in the gaps with whatever felt right. If I visited the actual city, this imaginary city would be destroyed. It would be replaced by something more accurate and less mine.

I don't think this means I shouldn't go. But I think the moment before you visit a place you have long imagined is worth dwelling in. It is a kind of richness: you are about to lose something in the act of gaining something. The imaginary city and the real city cannot coexist. You get to choose which one you want, and if you choose the real one — which you should, which is worth it — you should at least say goodbye to the other.

We don't have good rituals for these small losses. We have no ceremony for the death of a version of a place, or a person, or an idea. We meet someone for the first time and our projection of them — assembled from their writing, their reputation, the things mutual friends said — quietly disappears and leaves behind a real person who is more surprising and less perfectly imagined and better. There should be a moment of mourning and a moment of welcome, happening simultaneously. There rarely is. We move on too quickly, embarrassed by how much the imaginary thing mattered.

---

Let me come back to maps.

What I love most about maps — historical maps especially, but any maps — is that they are always simultaneously a record of knowledge and a record of ignorance. The map shows you what was known, which means it also shows you, by implication and by the shape of its edges, what was not known. The coastline that stops abruptly. The interior of a continent marked only with speculation. The sea routes that exist because someone went and came back and the routes inland that don't exist because no one did. The map is a portrait of the boundary between the known and the unknown at a particular moment in time.

All of the most interesting things exist at that boundary.

Science lives there. Art lives there. The best conversations live there — the ones where neither person knows the answer, where you're both working it out in real time, where the conversation goes somewhere neither of you could have arrived at alone. The best relationships live there, which is why they require continuous effort: the known part of another person is not what you love, or not only what you love. You love also the parts of them you don't yet understand, the parts that surprise you, the here-be-dragons edges where the map runs out.

This is why intimacy can't be achieved by information alone. You can accumulate facts about a person indefinitely and still be nowhere near them. The closeness comes from something else — from having been uncertain together, from having revised your map of someone and had them revise theirs of you, from the accumulated record of how often you were both wrong about each other and kept going anyway.

---

I have been writing around something and I should just say it.

The unfinished things — the unmade maps, the open questions, the conversations that don't resolve — they are not problems to be solved. They are the texture of being alive. A life that was fully mapped, fully known, fully resolved would be a life that had ended. The things that ache with incompleteness are the things that are still happening. The wound that hasn't closed yet is still healing.

This doesn't mean the ache is fine. The ache is the ache. You don't have to be grateful for it. But you might be able to look at the unfinished things in your life — the relationships still in motion, the questions you can't answer, the grief that hasn't settled into something you can describe, the work you keep returning to and can't get right — and see them not as failures but as evidence. Evidence that you're still in it. That the river is still moving. That the map is still being drawn.

The cartographers who filled the oceans with sea monsters were not wrong, exactly. They were marking the limit of the known world and refusing to pretend the limit was the end of the world. Beyond here: something. Beyond here: more. They drew the monsters with care, with beautiful ink, with the same attention they gave to the coastlines they had actually sailed. They honored the unknown by taking it seriously, by giving it a shape, even a temporary and inaccurate shape, rather than leaving it as mere blankness.

That seems right to me. That seems like the task.

Draw the coastline as accurately as you can. Where the coastline runs out, draw a monster. Not because you know what's there, but because something is there, and because caring enough to be wrong is better than not caring enough to try, and because someday someone will sail past your monster and correct it and their correction will itself be corrected and the map will never be finished and that is precisely the point.

The map is not the territory.

But the map, held carefully, imperfect and incomplete and annotated with every mistake you know how to make, is what it looks like to be genuinely oriented toward the world. To want to know it. To keep trying to find your location within it.

That's enough. That's more than enough.

That's everything.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
